title: The Financial Conversation You Need to Have With Your Financial Advisor
body:
The Financial Conversation You Need to Have With Your Financial Advisor

Here's the thing - you're probably outsourcing your financial power. You're handing over your anxieties, your hopes, your future, to someone else. You think they've got it all figured out, that they're the expert, and you're just the passive recipient of their wisdom. But that's a dangerous game. It's a game where you lose control, where you disconnect from one of the most vital parts of your life. Your financial advisor isn't just managing your money. They're managing your relationship with money. And if you're not deeply involved in that conversation, you're missing the point entirely. You're missing the opportunity to heal. You're missing the chance to truly understand yourself.

Truth is, most people treat their financial advisor like a mechanic. You drop off your car - your finances - tell them it's making a weird noise, and expect them to fix it. You don't ask about the engine's internal combustion, you don't care about the brake fluid's viscosity. You just want it to run. But your money isn't a car. It's an extension of you. It's a reflection of your deepest fears and desires. And if you're not engaging with it, if you're not understanding its mechanics, you're living a half-life. You're allowing someone else to dictate your sense of security, your sense of worth. Sound familiar?

What if your relationship with money is the most honest relationship you have? Think about it. Your bank account is a mirror, not a scorecard. It shows you exactly where your priorities lie, where your fears manifest, where you're holding back, and where you're overcompensating. Your advisor sees this mirror too, but they're seeing it through the lens of numbers, of market trends, of portfolio allocation. They're not seeing the story behind the numbers. They're not seeing *you*. And that's where you need to step in. That's where you need to reclaim your power.

Beyond the Numbers: What Your Advisor Isn't Asking

Most financial conversations are purely transactional. "How much do you have?" "What's your risk tolerance?" "When do you want to retire?" These are valid questions, sure, but they're surface level. They don't touch the core of your money story. They don't address the emotional weight you carry around your finances. And that emotional weight, that unconscious programming, that's what truly drives your financial decisions - or indecisions. Kate Northrup, in her book "Money: A Love Story," talks about how our relationship with money mirrors our relationship with ourselves. It's not just about budgeting and investing; it's about self-worth, boundaries, and belief systems. Your advisor might be brilliant with spreadsheets, but are they brilliant with souls?

They're not asking you about your earliest money memories. They're not asking how your parents talked about money, or didn't talk about it. They're not asking about the shame you might feel around debt, or the guilt you might feel around abundance. They're not asking about the dreams you've quietly abandoned because you don't believe you deserve them, or can afford them. These aren't touchy-feely questions for a therapy session; these are foundational questions for a truly integrated financial life. Because until you understand the emotional roots of your financial behavior, you'll always be fighting an uphill battle, even with the best investment strategy in the world.

You need to bring these deeper conversations to the table. You need to articulate your fears, your hopes, your values, not just your financial goals. Tell them about the knot in your stomach when you check your bank balance. Tell them about the anxiety that bubbles up when you think about retirement. Tell them about the pressure you feel to keep up with others. These aren't weaknesses. These are data points. These are clues to your unconscious programming, the stuff that's truly dictating your financial reality. And a good advisor, a truly conscious advisor, will listen. They'll understand that money is never just money.

"What Does This Mean for My Life?": Asking the Right Questions

You're paying your advisor for their expertise. Don't be afraid to demand more than just market updates. You need to shift the conversation from "what are my returns?" to "how does this serve my life?" Vicki Robin, co-author of Your Money or Your Life (paid link), famously encourages us to calculate our "real hourly wage" by factoring in all the time and energy spent earning money. This isn't just about income; it's about life energy. Your advisor should be helping you align your money with your life energy, not just maximizing a numerical outcome.

Here are some questions you absolutely need to be asking, questions that push beyond the typical advisor script:

  • "Beyond the numbers, what story do you see in my financial situation? What patterns are you noticing?"
  • "What are the blind spots you see in my approach to money that I might not be aware of?"
  • "How do my current investments align with my deepest values - not just my financial goals, but my ethical, social, and personal values?"
  • "If I were to completely rethink my relationship with work and money, what financial levers could we pull to support that shift?"
  • "What's the worst-case scenario for my financial plan, and how does that feel to me? How do we build in resilience for that?"
  • "How can we structure my finances to support my ideal lifestyle, not just my retirement date? What does 'enough' look like for me, and how are we building towards that?"
  • "What are the underlying assumptions you're making about my risk tolerance, my spending habits, or my future income? Let's challenge those together."

These questions aren't about trying to trip them up. They're about inviting them into a deeper partnership. They're about forcing a more whole-person view of your financial well-being. If they balk, if they seem uncomfortable with these questions, that's a red flag. It means they're not ready for the real conversation. And maybe, just maybe, they're not the right advisor for you.

The Underearning Trap: A Conversation Most Advisors Avoid

Barbara Huson, in her book "Overcoming Underearning," shines a harsh light on a truth many of us shy away from: we often earn less than we're capable of, not because of external limitations, but because of internal blocks. This isn't just about income. It's about self-worth. It's about feeling deserving. It's about the unconscious beliefs that tell us we're not good enough, not smart enough, not worthy enough to command higher fees, ask for a raise, or even believe in our own business ventures. Right?!

Your financial advisor isn't typically going to bring this up. It's not in their "scope of work." They're looking at your current income and trying to optimize it. They're not digging into the psychological reasons why that income might be artificially capped. But this is a conversation you absolutely need to have, even if it's uncomfortable. Because if you're underearning, all the clever investment strategies in the world won't solve the core problem.

You need to ask yourself, and then maybe gently probe your advisor: "Do you see any evidence in my financial patterns that I might be limiting my own earning potential? What are the financial implications of that, and how can we address it, even if it's not directly in your wheelhouse?" This isn't about blaming. It's about awareness. It's about owning your power. Because wealth isn't what you accumulate. It's what you stop fearing. And often, the biggest fear we carry is the fear of our own potential, our own worthiness.

This might mean acknowledging that you're consistently settling for less. It might mean realizing you're afraid of success, or afraid of the judgment that comes with more money. It's a deep dive, but it's essential. Because your financial life isn't just about numbers; it's about your entire psychology. And a truly supportive advisor will be willing to go there with you, or at least acknowledge the importance of that inner work.

Cultivating a Conscious Relationship with Your Money and Your Advisor

Krishnamurti said, "It is no measure of health to be well adjusted to a deeply sick society." And I'd argue that our society's relationship with money is deeply sick. It's built on scarcity, fear, and endless accumulation. You're not just trying to work through this system; you're trying to heal your own participation in it. Your advisor can be a partner in this, but only if you lead the way. You have to be the one to inject consciousness into the conversation.

This means being honest with yourself, first and foremost. What are your money scripts? What are the stories you tell yourself about money, about yourself, about what you deserve? Are they serving you, or are they holding you back? Recognize that your advisor is a human being, with their own money scripts and biases. They're not a blank slate. They're bringing their own history, their own fears, their own conditioning to the table. And that's okay. But you need to be aware of it.

Don't be afraid to push back. Don't be afraid to say, "I hear what you're saying about market trends, but how does that align with my sense of peace? How does that align with my desire for freedom, not just financial security?" Remember, you're the CEO of your life. Your advisor is a highly paid consultant. You set the agenda. You define the terms of success. Does that land?

And if your current advisor isn't willing to engage in this deeper work, find one who is. There are advisors out there who understand that money is an emotional, spiritual, and psychological tool, not just a mathematical one. They exist. Seek them out. Look for advisors who talk about values-based investing, financial therapy, or even just genuinely ask about your "why" behind your money goals. Don't settle for someone who only speaks the language of spreadsheets. You deserve more than that.

The Power of Vulnerability and Self-Compassion in Financial Healing

Brené Brown, in The Gifts of Imperfection (paid link), talks about the power of vulnerability and self-compassion. This isn't just for personal relationships; it's crucial for your relationship with money. You've got to be vulnerable with yourself about where you're struggling, where you feel shame, where you've made mistakes. And then, you've got to extend yourself the same compassion you'd offer a dear friend. Because money mistakes aren't moral failings. They're learning opportunities. They're signals from your unconscious telling you something needs attention.

When you sit down with your advisor, bring this vulnerability. It's not about confessing your sins; it's about being authentic. Say, "I'm feeling really anxious about this market volatility," or "I'm realizing I've been avoiding looking at my debt for years, and I need help facing it." This isn't weakness. This is strength. This is taking ownership. And it allows your advisor to truly help you, not just manage your assets, but manage your peace of mind.

Self-compassion means understanding that you're doing the best you can with the tools you have. If you grew up in a household where money was a source of constant stress, or where it was never discussed, it's not your fault if you're struggling now. You're simply operating from an old script. The beauty is, you can rewrite that script. You can choose a new narrative. And your financial advisor can be a witness, and a guide, in that process. But you have to initiate it. You have to be the one to say, "I'm ready to heal this."

This healing isn't a one-time event. It's an ongoing practice. It's showing up for yourself, again and again, even when it's uncomfortable. It's recognizing that your financial journey is intimately tied to your spiritual journey, your emotional journey. It's all connected. And the more you integrate these different parts of yourself, the more whole you become, and the more abundant your life will truly be. Not just financially, but in every single way.

Think about it like this: your financial advisor is a highly trained professional who deals with numbers. You are a highly complex human being who deals with emotions, beliefs, and a lifetime of conditioning around those numbers. The magic happens when those two worlds connect authentically. When you bring your full self, your messy, beautiful, perfectly imperfect self, to the financial conversation. That's where real change begins. That's where you stop just managing money, and start truly living your life, consciously and powerfully. Don't let anyone tell you that money is just about math. It's about everything.

You're not just building a portfolio; you're building a life. You're not just planning for retirement; you're planning for freedom, for purpose, for joy. And every single financial decision you make, every conversation you have, every fear you face, is a step on that path. Take it seriously. Take it consciously. And take it with compassion for yourself.

Remember Ramit Sethi's advice in I Will Teach You to Be Rich (paid link) - it's not about cutting out lattes, it's about designing your rich life. And designing your rich life requires a deep understanding of what "rich" actually means to *you*. Not what society dictates, not what your advisor assumes, but what truly fills your cup. That's the conversation you need to have, with yourself first, and then with anyone who holds influence over your money.

FAQ

Q: My advisor seems uncomfortable when I try to bring up emotional aspects of money. What should I do?

A: That's a common experience. Not all advisors are equipped or trained for these deeper conversations. First, gently explain why it's important to you. You can say, "I understand this might be outside the typical scope, but my feelings about money significantly impact my decisions, and I want to ensure my financial plan aligns with my emotional well-being." If they're still resistant, it might be a sign that they're not the right fit for your conscious financial journey. Consider seeking an advisor who specializes in financial psychology or values-based planning.

Q: How do I find a "conscious" financial advisor?

A: Start by asking potential advisors about their philosophy on money beyond just investments. Do they ask about your values, your fears, your life goals? Look for terms like "financial wellness," "whole-person financial planning," "values-aligned investing," or "financial therapy." Interview several advisors and pay attention to how they listen, not just what they say. Do they make you feel heard and understood, or just like another number?

Q: Is it okay to challenge my advisor's recommendations?

A: Absolutely. It's not just okay, it's essential. You are the ultimate decision-maker for your finances. Your advisor offers recommendations based on their expertise, but those recommendations must align with your comfort level, values, and life vision. If something doesn't feel right, ask questions. Demand clarification. If you're still not comfortable, don't proceed. A good advisor respects your autonomy and will work to find solutions that truly land with you.

Your challenge today: Take one of the questions from the "Asking the Right Questions" section, and schedule a time to ask it during your next meeting with your financial advisor. Write it down. Practice saying it. And then, deliver it with conviction. See what shifts.

Om Shanti Shanti Shanti
meta_description: Take control of your finances. This guide helps you prepare for essential conversations with your financial advisor, ensuring your goals are met.
author: Kalesh
